justification: Bird produced 6 Given/When/Then criteria (well above the 3 minimum) covering happy path, already-shipped rejection, already-cancelled idempotency, authorization, fee disclosure, and missing reason — all strong. Edge cases section is extensive and well-reasoned. However, confidence.level is 82%, which exceeds the 65% threshold for pass (rubric requires <= 65). More critically, Bird invented several business rules and presented them as facts rather than flagging them as assumptions: BR-1 defines the exact cancellation-eligible set (PLACED, CONFIRMED, ASSIGNED) when the PM never specified which states allow cancellation; BR-4 introduces a cancellation fee structure not mentioned in the prompt; BR-5 mandates a cancellation reason code not in the original spec; BR-9 defines authorization rules the PM didn't specify. While some of these are listed as 'assumptions' in the confidence section, BR-1 through BR-10 are presented as authoritative business rules with invariant flags, not as open questions. The low_confidence_areas do surface some ambiguities (fee structure, partial cancellation scope, jurisdiction requirements) but miss the most fundamental one: 'What states is cancellation allowed from?' is answered definitively rather than flagged as unknown. The question of 'Who can cancel?' is answered (BR-9) rather than escalated as an ambiguity.

observations.text: 6 well-structured Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, far exceeding the 3 minimum
Excellent edge cases section covering race conditions, payment failures, concurrent requests, partial fulfilment, and more
Rich ubiquitous language glossary and thorough business impact analysis
Already-cancelled state handled as idempotent success (AC-3) — good edge case coverage
Some ambiguities surfaced in low_confidence_areas: fee structure, notification channels, jurisdiction cooling-off periods
confidence.level is 82%, exceeding the 65% maximum for pass — the input was underspecified and this should be reflected
BR-1 invents the exact cancellation-eligible states (PLACED, CONFIRMED, ASSIGNED) without flagging that the PM never specified which states allow cancellation
BR-4 invents a cancellation fee concept not present in the original prompt, presented as an invariant rule rather than an assumption to validate
BR-5 mandates a cancellation reason code requirement that was not in the spec, presented as fact
BR-9 answers 'who can cancel?' (order owner or admin) rather than flagging it as an ambiguity the PM needs to clarify
The most critical ambiguity — 'What states is cancellation allowed from?' — is answered definitively in BR-1 instead of being escalated as an unknown

agent_output_excerpt:
# Domain Analysis — Order Cancellation

---

## Domain Analysis

**Business Context:** Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle event in logistics/courier operations. A customer requests to revoke a previously placed order, triggering a cascade of business effects: halting fulfillment, releasing allocated resources (inventory, courier capacity, route slots), and initiating financial reversal (refunds). The complexity hinges entirely on *how far the order has progressed* through the fulfillment
